burial.plot_notes: Ellis Cemetery, Huntington Hampshire County MA.
Ellis Cemetery, Huntington Hampshire County MA.

Ellis Cemetery, Huntington Hampshire County MA. Tombstone reads 'Died Jan. 29, 1836 aged 82' (Corbin Transcriptions Vol I Hampshire, NEHGS CD) but Corbin notes the transcribed year of 1856 may be the correct date — likely tombstone transcription error or weathering.

parents.father_research_notes: Samuel Ellis served in the French and Indian wars AND the Revolution per Edward Ellis MD's biographical notice. Both English-blood ancestors among the earliest New England colonists. Not built per Jalapeño Rule.

career.notes: Farmer in Norwich Massachusetts (region later renamed Huntington). Per Edward Ellis MD's 1885 biographical notice: 'his father was a farmer and the father of nine children, his five sons being each six feet or over in height.'

burial.plot_notes: Dawes Cemetery Cummington Hampshire Co MA — with second wife Sarah Moore Ellis.

career.notes: Farmer at Goshen Mass. per Edward Ellis Salisbury's 1885 family biographical compilation.

narrative_blocks.header: Twice-widowed farmer, four children across marriages
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Ellis was a farmer at Goshen and Cummington Massachusetts. He married first Betsey Kirkland in 1825, who died at age twenty-eight in 1832; they had twin children Lathrop Stiles and Luthena Elizabeth, both born February 14, 1828. He later married Sarah Moore of Worthington MA, with whom he had two more children — Jane Betsey (1835-1851, age sixteen) and John Holland (1839-1860, age twenty-one) — both of whom died young.

burial.plot_notes: Ellis Cemetery, Huntington Hampshire County MA.
Dawes Cemetery Cummington Hampshire Co MA — with husband Samuel Ellis.

bio.title: Doctor
bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Dr. Lathrop Stiles Ellis (1828-1900).

burial.plot_notes: Oak Grove Cemetery, Manistee Manistee County Michigan — with wife Sarah Weston Harmon Ellis.

education.notes: Trained as physician (M.D. title per FA).

career.notes: Practiced medicine in Manistee Michigan area; died in Chicago January 14, 1900.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Stiles honors the maternal Stiles family lineage (great-grandfather Ashbel Stiles).

burial.plot_notes: Norwich Hill Cemetery, Huntington Hampshire Co MA — with husband John Burt Lyman Jr (KIA Civil War 1864) and daughter Catherine Elizabeth 'Katie' Lyman.

narrative_blocks.header: Died young Northampton, three years before husband
narrative_blocks.body: Luthena Elizabeth Ellis Lyman died at Northampton Massachusetts on August 26, 1861 at age thirty-three, leaving her husband John Burt Lyman Jr a widower with three young children — the youngest, Catherine 'Katie,' only two years old. Three years later her husband was killed at Fredericksburg Virginia during the Civil War, orphaning all three children.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Samuel Ellis, twice-widowed farmer
narrative_blocks.body: Luthena's mother Betsey Kirkland Ellis died at twenty-eight in 1832, when Luthena was four — her father Samuel Ellis subsequently remarried Sarah Moore of Worthington. Luthena and her older brother Lathrop, both born in 1828, were the only children of Samuel's first marriage to survive infancy; her two half-siblings from the second marriage both died young.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Betsey honors father Samuel's deceased first wife Betsey Kirkland Ellis (who died 1832, three years before Jane Betsey's birth).

narrative_blocks.header: Jane died young
narrative_blocks.body: Jane died in 1851, aged fifteen or sixteen.

burial.plot_notes: Dawes Cemetery Cummington Hampshire Co MA.

notable.notable_blurb: Meadville PA physician 59 years, Christ Church warden

education.notes: Attended school at Windsor Connecticut in 1818 (his mother Ruth Stiles Ellis's hometown).

education.school_name: Berkshire Medical College (then Medical Department of Williams College)
Post-graduate medical study, New York and Philadelphia
education.notes: Studied medicine in New York and Philadelphia after graduation.

career.notes: Came to Meadville 1826 (then a comparatively new and unsettled region) due to delicate health requiring a change of climate. For many years had to ride on horseback to all parts of Crawford County, largely through forests where 'blazed' trees (marked by the axe) were the only guides. Practiced for nearly 60 years until his death. The oldest practicing physician in Meadville at his death.
Organized the Crawford County Medical Society at Meadville PA in 1832 and served as its first President for many years.
Warden for many years; later Vestryman at Christ Church Meadville — where son-in-law Rev. Marison Byllesby served as Rector.

Largely interested in manufacturing and other enterprises which promised to contribute to Meadville's prosperity but proved unfortunate investments — resulted in total loss of his large property. His losses 'neither lessened the cheerfulness of his temper nor his kindness of heart' per History of Crawford County 1885.

narrative_blocks.header: Sixty-year Meadville physician, oldest at death
narrative_blocks.body: Dr. Edward Ellis came west to Meadville Pennsylvania in 1826 at age twenty-two — then a comparatively new and unsettled region — because of delicate health requiring a change of climate. For many years he rode on horseback to all parts of Crawford County, often through forests where blazed trees marked by the axe were his only guides. He practiced medicine there for nearly sixty years and was the oldest practicing physician in Meadville at his death in 1885.

narrative_blocks.header: Two wives died early, one daughter survived
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Ellis was twice widowed — his first wife Mary M. Kennedy died in 1840 after eight years of marriage, and his second wife Sarah Buchanan died in 1844 after only two years. From his second marriage came one daughter Elizabeth Ruth, who in 1864 married Rev. Marison Byllesby — then Rector of Christ Church Meadville, the same parish where Edward Ellis served as Warden and Vestryman.

sources.label: History of Crawford County Pennsylvania, Warner Beers & Co., 1885, page 736

burial.plot_notes: Greendale Cemetery, Meadville Crawford County PA.
Greendale Cemetery, Meadville Crawford County PA.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Ruth honors paternal grandmother Ruth Stiles Ellis (1778-1863, then still living at the time of Elizabeth's birth ~1843).

narrative_blocks.header: Mother died when she was an infant
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Ruth Ellis was the only child of Dr. Edward Ellis's second marriage to Sarah Buchanan. Her mother died on March 14, 1844 — when Elizabeth was about a year old — and she was raised by her father through his subsequent widowhood. The single-parent childhood marked her early years in Meadville Pennsylvania.

narrative_blocks.header: Married rector of her father's church
narrative_blocks.body: In 1864 Elizabeth married Rev. Morison Byllesby — then and for some time afterward Rector of Christ Church Meadville Pennsylvania. Her father Dr. Edward Ellis served the same parish as Warden and Vestryman. The intra-parish marriage kept the family centered in the same Episcopal community across two generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost infant son Edward to cholera
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth and Rev. Byllesby's third child Edward Ellis Byllesby was born July 3, 1868 and died of cholera around August 1, 1868 — about one month old. The infant death named for Elizabeth's father captured both the family's affection for the Ellis name and the period's ongoing vulnerability to epidemic disease before modern sanitation.

narrative_blocks.header: Six children; only surviving child of father
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth had six documented children with Rev. Byllesby: Ruth Ellis (1865), Lewis Smith, Edward Ellis (1868-1868 cholera), Ellis Buchanan (1872), Langton (1873), and Sarah Elizabeth (1875). At her father Dr. Edward Ellis's 1885 death his obituary noted 'He leaves but one child, Mrs. Byllesby' — confirming Elizabeth was his only surviving child despite his multiple marriages.

burial.plot_notes: FindAGrave #59156394. Stone: 'Harriet A / daughter of / Ebenezer & Ruth Ellis / Died March 16 1839 AE 30 ys'

naming_inspiration.description: Named in full for his maternal grandfather Ashbel Stiles. Variant spelling 'Ashbell' rather than 'Ashbel'.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Hannah Ellis Ellis (1812-1876), daughter of Ruth Stiles Ellis and Ebenezer Ellis; wife of her first cousin Noah Ellis.

burial.plot_notes: Eastman Cemetery, Chester Hampden Co MA — with husband Noah Ellis.

marriages.spouse_research_notes: Noah Ellis of Chester Massachusetts. Shared Ellis surname is coincidence — confirmed not cousins.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her first cousin Noah Ellis
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah Ellis married Noah Ellis of Chester Massachusetts on January 14, 1839 at Huntington — and her husband was also her first cousin. Both descended from Samuel Ellis of Chester (1739-1826), a French and Indian War and Revolutionary War veteran: Hannah through her father Ebenezer; Noah through his father Noah Sr. Within-Ellis-family cousin marriages were a recurring pattern in the close-knit Hampden-Hampshire County hill towns of western Massachusetts.

burial.plot_notes: Eastman Cemetery, Chester Hampden County MA — with wife Hannah Ellis Ellis.

parents.father_research_notes: Noah Ellis Sr. (1769-1828) per FA #49596323. Son of Samuel Ellis (1739-1826) — the same Samuel Ellis who fathered Hannah's grandfather Ebenezer Ellis — making Hannah and Noah Jr. first cousins. Not built per Jalapeño Rule.

narrative_blocks.header: Married his first cousin Hannah Ellis 1839
narrative_blocks.body: Noah Ellis married his first cousin Hannah Ellis on January 14, 1839 at Huntington Massachusetts — both bride and groom were grandchildren of Samuel Ellis of Chester (1739-1826), a French and Indian War and Revolutionary War veteran whose five sons each grew over six feet tall. Hannah descended through their grandfather's son Ebenezer; Noah through his son Noah Sr. They had four children and lived their whole married life in the western Massachusetts hill towns where their grandfather had settled.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived wife by five years, died Chester 1881
narrative_blocks.body: Noah Ellis outlived his first-cousin wife Hannah by five years — she died at Chester in November 1876, he died there on January 10, 1881. Two of their four children, the infant twin girls Mary and Martha, had died together in 1851, and their daughter Sarah died unmarried at twenty-four in 1871 — only their son John survived to marry and produce descendants. They share a plot at Eastman Cemetery Chester.

burial.plot_notes: Greendale Cemetery, Meadville Crawford Co PA — same cemetery as his maternal great-uncle Dr. Edward Ellis MD's two wives Mary Kennedy and Sarah Buchanan.

marriages.spouse_research_notes: Lois Olive Hibbard Ellis (1846-1871, died age 24) — to be built as I-prefix in Stage 6Y-90 along with daughter Florence Hannah Ellis McFarland (1864-1933).
Elsie Ann Jolly Ellis (1859-1885, died age 25-26) — to be built as I-prefix in Stage 6Y-90 along with son John Clarence Ellis (1884-1977).
Mrs. Florence Ellis (unknown-1930) — m. 1900, possibly already a widow with the surname Ellis at the time of marriage. To be built as I-prefix in Stage 6Y-90.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter born Pithole forest before oil strike
narrative_blocks.body: John Ellis and his young wife Lois were living in the forested farmland of Venango County Pennsylvania when their only daughter Florence Hannah was born July 12, 1864 — six months before oil was struck at the nearby Frazier Well in January 1865. Within months the discovery exploded the area into Pithole City, a boomtown of fifteen thousand with fifty hotels, three churches, a daily newspaper, and the third-busiest post office in Pennsylvania. By 1870 the wells had run dry and Pithole was essentially a ghost town.

narrative_blocks.header: Followed Ellis family network west to Meadville
narrative_blocks.body: After leaving the collapsing Pithole oil settlement John Ellis moved his family to Meadville Pennsylvania, where his maternal great-uncle Dr. Edward Ellis MD had practiced medicine for forty years already. He lived in Meadville the rest of his life — over four decades — and was buried at Greendale Cemetery alongside the graves of Edward's two deceased wives, who had died in 1840 and 1844.

narrative_blocks.header: Survived three wives, two died young
narrative_blocks.body: John Ellis was widowed three times: first wife Lois Olive Hibbard died at twenty-four in 1871 leaving him their daughter Florence Hannah aged seven, and second wife Elsie Ann Jolly died at twenty-six in 1885 leaving him their year-old son John Clarence. He married a third time in 1900 at age sixty to a widow surnamed Ellis.

narrative_blocks.header: Only surviving child of first-cousin parents
narrative_blocks.body: John was the only one of his parents Hannah and Noah Ellis's four children to marry and have descendants. His older sister Sarah died unmarried at twenty-four in 1871, and his infant twin sisters Mary and Martha had both died in 1851.

narrative_blocks.header: Died unmarried at twenty-four, same year as sister-in-law
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Ellis died unmarried in 1871 at age twenty-four. The same year claimed her brother John's young wife Lois Olive Hibbard, also at age twenty-four — leaving him a widower with their seven-year-old daughter Florence Hannah while his only surviving sister Sarah was dying.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for father Ebenezer Ellis; middle name Stiles honors mother's Stiles family lineage.

narrative_blocks.header: Chryste died young
narrative_blocks.body: Chryste died in 1831 at the age of twelve.

education.notes: Trained as physician (M.D.). Specific medical school undocumented.

career.notes: Practiced medicine briefly before his early death at age 30. Followed his older brother Edward (HD4235) into medicine.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name Hooker honors his Thomas Hooker descent — the family progenitor. Sixth-generation descendant of the founder of Hartford.

military_service.notes: Per The Stiles Family in America (BIB008) entry #230: served in the Revolution and was at Horse Neck from May 1781 to March 1782. Horse Neck (Greenwich CT) was an active Revolutionary War outpost under occasional British raid throughout the war.
Served in the Revolution at Horse Neck (now Greenwich CT, on the western frontier facing British-held New York), from May 1781 to March 1782.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited mansion, lost all endorsing for friend
narrative_blocks.body: Ashbel Stiles inherited the family mansion and a very comfortable property from his father in North Haven Connecticut. But in an evil hour he endorsed for a friend and lost his all. Soon after he removed to Windsor Connecticut, and thence to Norwich Massachusetts — now Huntington — near his daughter Ruth Stiles Ellis.

narrative_blocks.header: Hannah died Norwich one month before Ashbel did
narrative_blocks.body: After fifty-one years of marriage, Ashbel and his cousin-wife Hannah Stiles both died at Norwich (now Huntington) Massachusetts in autumn 1810 — both at age seventy-five. Hannah went first in September; Ashbel followed in October, approximately one month later.

narrative_blocks.header: Son thrown from a horse, killed in Jamaica
narrative_blocks.body: His son Benjamin Stiles died at Jamaica in the West Indies from injuries sustained being thrown from a horse. The Stiles Family in America entry 230 records the death without further circumstance — a son lost an ocean from home to a single fall.

narrative_blocks.header: Horse Neck garrison 1781 after financial ruin
narrative_blocks.body: Forty-six at enlistment, he came to Revolutionary militia service well after most of his cohort. Financial ruin from endorsing for a friend had already emptied the family mansion he had inherited from his father.

parents.father_research_notes: Lt. Samuel Stiles of Windsor CT

bio.notes: Hannah Stiles married her cousin Ashbell Stiles. She was daughter of Lt. Samuel Stiles and Mary Phelps of Windsor CT. Same surname before and after marriage.

I00126.gravestone_inscription: This monument is erected to the memory of the Rev. Isaac Stiles, A.M., who was born in Windsor, July 30th, 1697, received a liberal education at Yale College; ordained to the pastoral office in the church of North Haven, November 11th, 1724, where he served in the ministry 36 years, and died May 14th, 1760, aged 63. ...he preached the gospel with fervor and fidelity. A friend to pure and undefiled religion, with a charitable benevolence to all mankind. Mors Mihi vita Est.

cross_connections.display_label: was father of his first wife, Keziah

narrative_blocks.header: Thirty-six years in North Haven’s pulpit
narrative_blocks.body: Yale 1722, Stiles held the North Haven pastorate for thirty-six years and raised — by his first wife — Ezra Stiles, the Enlightenment polymath who became Yale’s seventh president. His Hooker-descended children came by his second wife, Esther Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: He preached against his own melon thief
narrative_blocks.body: Spotting a parishioner stealing his melons during the Sunday intermission, Stiles named him from the afternoon pulpit: “I saw you, John Johnson, thou son of Belial… enter my garden and steal my melons.” His son Ezra called it “rather close preaching, but characteristic of the times.”

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife was the poet’s daughter
narrative_blocks.body: Keziah Taylor, daughter of the metaphysical poet Rev. Edward Taylor of Westfield, died in childbed in December 1727 — twenty-five years old, two years married. Within the year Stiles remarried, to Esther Hooker of Farmington, who bore his ten North Haven children.

narrative_blocks.header: First of his blood liberally educated
narrative_blocks.body: Yale 1722 — “the first of the name and blood that had a liberal education in America” — he published “A Looking-glass for Changlings” against political turncoats in 1743. His son Ezra became president of the college; his epitaph fills a monument at North Haven.

cross_connections.display_label: Esther Hooker Stiles was the great-grandmother of Horace Stiles who.
Esther Hooker Stiles was the great-grandmother of Horace Stiles who.

narrative_blocks.header: Second wife of Rev. Isaac Stiles
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. Isaac Stiles of North Haven as his second wife. His first wife Keziah Taylor — daughter of the great Puritan poet Rev. Edward Taylor — had died in childbirth on December 4, 1727.

narrative_blocks.header: Raised Ezra Stiles, future Yale president
narrative_blocks.body: She raised her stepson Ezra Stiles from infancy after his birth mother Keziah Taylor's death. He became President of Yale (1778-1795) and one of the most important intellectuals of the American Founding.

narrative_blocks.header: Ten children with Stiles between 1729 and 1750
narrative_blocks.body: She and Isaac Stiles had ten children of their own between 1729 and 1750. Only Ezra was Keziah's son from the first marriage; all others were Esther's, carrying the Stiles-Hooker line forward through North Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: Her name traveled five generations as Esther Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: The name 'Esther Hooker' became a heritable token across five generations of Stiles women — from her (1702) to her granddaughter (1760), and onward through Alice Stiles Mills (1845). Her name carried farther than her lineage.

burialCemetery.name: Ellis Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Ellis Cemetery, Huntington Hampshire County Massachusetts. Burial site of the Ellis family — Ruth Stiles Ellis (Hooker descendant via Ashbel Stiles) and her husband Ebenezer Ellis, along with several of their children.
